I hold to the Reformed Baptist position because the Scriptures describe human beings as "dead in trespasses and sins," implying that we are unable to choose God unless He first grants us a new heart and a new spirit (Ephesians 2:1–5; Ezekiel 36:26–27). This doctrine, often called the "perseverance of the saints," provides great comfort by grounding our security in the unwavering faithfulness of Christ rather than the strength of our own grip on Him (John 10:28–29).

Whether one leans toward the Arminian or Reformed tradition, the practice of feet washing is considered a tertiary matter where faithful Christians may disagree. Some see it as a mandatory third ordinance because of Christ's direct command in the Upper Room, while others view it as a cultural example of humility that is fulfilled today through various acts of practical service (Romans 14:5). Both views seek to honor the spirit of Christ’s servanthood.

If you find yourself wrestling with the deep questions of God's sovereignty and your own choices, remember that the most important truth is that God calls you today to trust in His Son. I encourage you to bring these questions to the Lord in prayer and to seek the counsel of a local, Bible-believing church where you can study the Word in community.

How does the reality of God’s grace impact the way you view your own daily walk with Him?

question: What does it mean that you are the head and not the tail in Deuteronomy 28:13?
answer:
In Deuteronomy 28:13, being the "head and not the tail" refers to a position of leadership, honor, and priority that God promised to the nation of Israel, conditional upon their total obedience to His commandments. To be the head implies being the one who leads and sets the direction, while being the tail suggests being a follower or living in subjection to the will and power of other nations.

This promise is situated within the blessings and curses of the Mosaic Covenant. It illustrates God’s desire to distinguish His people as a "holy people to Himself" if they remained faithful to His Word (Deuteronomy 28:9). However, we must handle this text with great care and pastoral plainness. While it speaks of national prominence for ancient Israel, it ultimately points us to our deep need for a perfect representative. The reality of human history shows that Israel, like all of us, failed to keep the law perfectly and often found themselves as the "tail" because of their wandering hearts.

Theologically, this promise finds its ultimate fulfillment in the **active obedience** of Jesus Christ. Because Christ obeyed the Father perfectly in every point where we have failed, He is the supreme Head over all things (Ephesians 1:22). Through faith, the believer is united to Him. This means your primary identity is no longer defined by your earthly status, your failures, or your social rank. In Christ, you are no longer a slave to sin or a "tail" to the whims of this falling world; you are a child of the King, spiritually seated with Him in heavenly places (Ephesians 2:6).

We must be cautious not to twist this verse into a "prosperity gospel" that guarantees worldly wealth or social dominance. The blessings of the New Covenant are primarily spiritual and eternal. While God in His providence may grant success in this life, the greatest blessing is our **justification**—being declared righteous before a holy God because of what Jesus has done. He took the "curse" of the law upon Himself on the cross so that we might receive the blessing of the Spirit through faith (Galatians 3:13–14).

If you are currently feeling like the "tail"—perhaps overwhelmed by debt, struggling with health, or feeling overlooked—I encourage you to look away from your circumstances and toward Christ. Your worth is not measured by where you sit in the world's hierarchy, but by where you stand in God's grace. 

**A Practical Step for You:**
This week, read through Deuteronomy 28:1–14 and then immediately read Galatians 3. As you pray, do not ask God to make you the "head" in a worldly sense of power, but ask Him to help you live in the reality of your high calling in Christ. Thank Him that because of Jesus, you are an heir to every spiritual blessing.

If you have never placed your trust in Jesus, I invite you to turn from the heavy burden of trying to earn God’s favor. We are all sinners separated from a holy God, but Jesus Christ, the Son of God, lived perfectly, died for our sins, and rose bodily from the grave to give us new life. Trust in Him alone for your salvation today, and you will find a rest and a status that the world can never take away.

question: What is theistic agnosticism / agnostic theism?
answer:
Agnostic theism, or theistic agnosticism, is the philosophical stance of those who believe that at least one deity exists but also maintain that the existence or nature of God is ultimately unknown or unknowable. It is a position that distinguishes between "belief" (theism) and "knowledge" (gnosticism), where the individual chooses to have faith in a Creator while acknowledging that they lack logical or empirical certainty.

This perspective often arises from a place of intellectual humility, recognizing that the human mind is finite and limited. A person might feel that if a supreme Being exists, that Being must be so transcendent and far above our physical reality that any claim to "know" God for certain would be an overreach of human ability.

While Christians share a deep reverence for the transcendence and the **incomprehensibility** of God—meaning we can never fully or exhaustively wrap our minds around His infinite nature—the Bible teaches that God has not remained silent or hidden. We believe in **Divine Revelation**, the truth that God has intentionally made Himself known to us. Scripture suggests that God’s existence is evident through the things He has made in Romans 1:19–20, and more specifically, He has revealed His character and His will through the written Word and the Person of Jesus Christ.

You may find it difficult to reconcile the vastness of the universe with the idea that we can truly know its Maker. It can feel as though claiming to know God is a form of spiritual pride. However, from a biblical perspective, knowing God is not a feat of human intellect, but a response to His grace. The Bible tells us in Hebrews 1:1–2 that while God spoke in many ways in the past, He has "in these last days spoken to us by His Son."

The heart of the Gospel is that the "Unknown God" has become known. Jesus Christ, who is truly God and truly man, entered our history, lived a perfect life, and died a substitutionary death on the cross for our sins. His bodily resurrection proves that He is Lord over life and death. Because of what Jesus has done, we are invited not just to believe that a God exists, but to enter into a personal, saving relationship with Him. In John 17:3, Jesus says, "Now this is eternal life: that they may know You, the only true God, and Jesus Christ, whom You have sent."

Salvation is found by turning from our own efforts and uncertainty and placing our trust in Christ alone for the forgiveness of our sins. I invite you to move beyond the "unknown" and talk to God directly. Ask Him to reveal the truth of His Word to you as you read. A wonderful place to start is the Gospel of John, which was written specifically so that you may believe that Jesus is the Christ and have life in His name as stated in John 20:31.

Have you ever spent time reading the accounts of Jesus' life to see how He reveals the character of God?

question: The Fruit of the Holy Spirit – What is kindness?
answer:
Kindness, as listed among the fruit of the Spirit, is the outward expression of a heart transformed by God’s grace, manifesting as a gentle, useful, and benevolent concern for the well-being of others. The Greek word used in the New Testament is *chrēstotēs*, which describes a goodness that is not merely an abstract quality but is active and helpful in its nature (Galatians 5:22).

In the ancient world, this term was often used to describe wine that had grown mellow and lost its harshness with age. Similarly, the Holy Spirit works a mellowing in the soul of a believer, smoothing over the sharp edges of a prickly or bitter temperament and replacing them with a tender disposition. This is a vital part of our progressive sanctification, where the Spirit increasingly conforms us to the image of Christ, who is the perfect embodiment of kindness.

This kindness is not a natural human "niceness" but is rooted in the character of God Himself. Scripture tells us that "when the kindness and love of God our Savior toward man appeared, He saved us, not by works of righteousness which we have done, but according to His mercy" (Titus 3:4-5). We see this divine kindness most clearly in the Gospel, for it is the kindness of God that leads us to repentance (Romans 2:4). While we were still sinners and enemies of God, He reached out to us in Christ to offer forgiveness and adoption into His family.

Because we have been the recipients of such overwhelming mercy, we are called to reflect that same kindness to a world that is often harsh and unforgiving. This means being "kind to one another, tenderhearted, forgiving one another, even as God in Christ forgave you" (Ephesians 4:32). True biblical kindness is not weak; it is a robust and courageous choice to act for the benefit of another, even at a cost to ourselves.

If you desire to see this fruit grow in your life, start by reflecting on the kindness Jesus has shown to you. I encourage you to choose one person this week who is difficult to love and perform a specific, practical act of service for them. As you do, ask the Lord in prayer to soften your heart and help you see them through His eyes of compassion. 

Have you found it difficult lately to show kindness to someone in your life, or are you seeking to understand how God’s kindness specifically applies to a burden you are carrying?

question: How should Christians view the idea of reparations for slavery?
answer:
The question of reparations for slavery is a sensitive and significant matter that touches the very heart of biblical justice, the dignity of those made in the image of God, and the complex nature of sin and its effects through generations. Christians should approach this topic with a deep sense of humility, recognizing that the institution of chattel slavery was a grievous sin—a theft of life, labor, and personhood that violated the clear commands of God (Exodus 21:16; 1 Timothy 1:10).

In seeking to understand how to respond to this history, Christians generally look to two primary biblical principles that seem to pull in different directions, requiring great wisdom and charity to navigate.

The first principle is that of restitution. In the Law of Moses, God required that when someone was wronged or their property was stolen, the offender was to make restitution to ensure the victim was made whole (Exodus 22:1). We see this principle beautifully lived out in the New Testament when Zacchaeus, after meeting Jesus, pledged to restore fourfold to anyone he had defrauded (Luke 19:8). From this perspective, many Christians argue that since slavery was a systemic theft of generational wealth and opportunity, some form of corporate or national restitution is a requirement of biblical justice.

The second principle is that of individual accountability. Scripture also teaches that a person is primarily responsible for their own sins and should not be punished for the sins of their ancestors (Deuteronomy 24:16; Ezekiel 18:20). Believers who emphasize this point often express concern that requiring current generations to pay for the specific crimes of their forefathers may inadvertently create a different form of perceived injustice, as it detaches the penalty from the personal agency of the one paying.

Because the Bible does not give a direct command concerning how a modern nation should settle the debts of a defunct historical institution, this is considered a tertiary issue where faithful Christians may disagree on the specific implementation of justice. 

1. Some believers argue for direct financial reparations, believing that the corporate nature of a nation or an institution carries the debts of its past, much as Daniel confessed the sins of his fathers (Daniel 9:16). 
2. Other believers advocate for "reparative justice," which focuses on addressing current systemic disparities through targeted investment in education, housing, and economic opportunity, rather than direct cash payments.
3. Still others believe that the focus should remain on individual heart change and local church reconciliation, fearing that political solutions may fail to address the underlying spiritual wounds.

In all these views, the Christian must never lose sight of the *imago Dei*—the truth that every human being possesses inherent worth because they are created by God (Genesis 1:27). Our ultimate hope is found in the Gospel of Jesus Christ. While we must strive for justice on earth, we recognize that the greatest debt ever owed was our sin against a holy God. This debt was one we could never pay, but Christ, in His mercy, paid it in full through His substitutionary death on the cross (Colossians 2:13–14). 

As a practical next step, I encourage you to study the history of your own local community or church to see how the legacy of slavery may have shaped its present reality. Ask God for a heart that "does justice, loves mercy, and walks humbly" (Micah 6:8). You might also consider inviting a brother or sister from a different ethnic background to share their perspective on this issue over a meal, listening with the intent to bear one another's burdens (Galatians 6:2).

Does your interest in this topic stem from a specific discussion in your church or community, or are you wrestling with how the Bible defines justice more broadly?

question: Who are we to pray to, the Father, the Son, or the Holy Spirit?
answer:
The biblical pattern for prayer is that we address God the Father, through the mediation of the Son, by the help of the Holy Spirit. Because each Person of the Trinity is fully and eternally God, sharing the same divine essence, it is also biblically appropriate to address the Son or the Spirit directly in your petitions and praise.

Our Lord Jesus taught us to pray, "Our Father in heaven, hallowed be Your name" in Matthew 6:9. This establishes the Father as the primary object of our adoption and our prayers. We approach Him not on our own merit, but through the "new and living way" opened for us by Jesus Christ, our great High Priest, as stated in Hebrews 10:19–22. Jesus encouraged His disciples to ask the Father for anything in His name, signifying that He is our necessary Mediator in John 16:23.

Furthermore, the Holy Spirit is the one who empowers our prayers, interceding for us with groanings too deep for words when we do not know how to pray as we ought, according to Romans 8:26. While the New Testament predominantly shows believers praying to the Father, we also see examples of direct address to the Son. For instance, as Stephen was being stoned, he cried out, "Lord Jesus, receive my spirit" in Acts 7:59. Similarly, the final cry of the Bible is a direct prayer to Christ: "Amen. Come, Lord Jesus!" in Revelation 22:20.

In your personal devotion, do not feel restricted by a rigid formula, for you are in fellowship with the Triune God. You might find it helpful to begin your prayers by consciously acknowledging this Trinitarian reality: thank the Father for His sovereign grace, thank the Son for His redeeming blood, and ask the Holy Spirit to guide your heart into all truth.

As a practical step this week, try reading through the prayers of the Apostle Paul in Ephesians 1:15–23 and Ephesians 3:14–21. Use his language to shape your own conversations with God, noticing how he directs his heart to the Father while grounding every blessing in the work of Christ and the power of the Spirit.

question: Why does God hate sin?
answer:
God hates sin because it is fundamentally contrary to His holy nature and acts as a destructive force that separates His image-bearers from the source of all life. Because God is perfectly good, He must possess a holy hatred for everything that opposes that goodness and causes ruin to His creation (Habakkuk 1:13; Psalm 5:4–5).

To understand this, we must look at the character of God. The Bible describes God as "holy, holy, holy," which is a biblical way of showing the highest degree of absolute purity and being set apart from all that is common or defiled (Isaiah 6:3; Revelation 4:8). The term holiness implies that God is light and in Him is no darkness at all. Sin, by definition, is lawlessness and a rebellion against God’s sovereign authority; it is an assault on His glory. As a perfectly just Judge, God cannot simply overlook sin, for to do so would be to deny His own righteous character (Exodus 34:7).

The tragedy of sin is that it brings death and spiritual separation, leaving us in a state of moral corruption where we cannot save ourselves (Romans 3:23; Romans 6:23). Yet, because God is also merciful and loving, He provided a way for us to be rescued. He sent His Son, Jesus Christ, who took on human nature to live the perfect life we could not live. Jesus then died a substitutionary death on the cross, taking the penalty we deserved to satisfy God's righteous justice. This is the heart of the Gospel: that through Christ's death and bodily resurrection, the very sin God hates can be forgiven, and we can be reconciled to Him (2 Corinthians 5:21; 1 Peter 3:18).

If you feel the weight of your own sin today, remember that God’s hatred for sin is matched by His infinite mercy toward the repentant. Turn from your sin and trust in Jesus Christ alone, who died and rose for you, to receive full forgiveness and eternal life.

As a practical step in your walk with God, I encourage you to read the first three chapters of the book of Romans. These chapters explain the depth of our human need and the wonderful gift of justification by grace through faith. Would you like to share what prompted your question about God's view of sin?

question: What will we look like in Heaven?
answer:
In eternity, we will possess physical, resurrected bodies that are patterned after the glorious body of our Lord Jesus Christ. While the Bible does not provide a detailed biological description, it assures us that our future state involves a total "glorification," which is the final step of our redemption where our bodies are made perfect, immortal, and fully suited for dwelling in the presence of God.

The most definitive teaching on our heavenly appearance comes from the resurrection of Jesus Himself. After He rose from the dead, He was not a wandering spirit or a ghostly apparition; He had "flesh and bones" and could eat food, yet He was no longer bound by the limitations of the fall (Luke 24:39-43; John 20:19-20). Scripture tells us that "we shall be like Him, for we shall see Him as He is" (1 John 3:2). This means that just as Christ was recognizable to His disciples, you will still be "you," but a version of yourself that is completely liberated from sin, decay, and physical frailty.

The Apostle Paul provides a beautiful contrast between our current bodies and our future ones in his first letter to the Corinthians. He explains that "what is sown is perishable, it is raised imperishable; it is sown in dishonor, it is raised in glory; it is sown in weakness, it is raised in power; it is sown a natural body, it is raised a spiritual body" (1 Corinthians 15:42-44). In this context, a "spiritual body" does not mean a non-physical one, but rather a body fully empowered and directed by the Holy Spirit, no longer subject to the "lowly" conditions of this present age (Philippians 3:21).

Whether we will appear at a certain age or with specific physical traits from our earthly life is a matter where faithful Christians may hold different thoughts, as the Bible is silent on these minor details. Some suggest we may appear in the "prime" of life, while others believe our unique earthly features will be perfected rather than replaced. What remains certain is that every trace of the curse—every illness, disability, and scar of age—will be wiped away.

This glorious hope is not a reward for our own efforts, but a gift purchased by the blood of Christ. Because of our sin, we were separated from God and subject to death, but Christ took our judgment upon Himself on the cross and rose bodily from the grave to secure our victory. This transformation is only for those who have turned from their sin and placed their trust in Jesus Christ alone for their salvation.

If you are currently struggling with physical pain, illness, or the effects of aging, let this truth be a balm to your soul. Your current body is like a seed, and though it may seem weak now, God promises to raise it in unimaginable power and beauty.

A helpful way to reflect on this is to spend time this week reading 1 Corinthians 15 and thanking God for the specific ways He will bring healing to your body in the resurrection. Do you find comfort in knowing that your future body will be free from the pains and limitations you feel today?

question: What is sola scriptura?
answer:
Sola Scriptura is the conviction that the Holy Scriptures are the sole infallible rule of faith and practice for the Christian and the church. It teaches that while God has kindly given us pastors, historical wisdom, and reason, the Bible stands as the supreme and final authority by which all other claims must be measured.

This truth is a great mercy to us, for it ensures that our consciences are bound only to the voice of God and not to the fluctuating opinions or traditions of men. The term is Latin for Scripture alone and served as a vital anchor during the Reformation, emphasizing that Scripture is *theopneustos* (God-breathed) and therefore carries a divine weight that no human institution can equal (2 Timothy 3:16–17). Just as the Bereans were honored for testing the preaching of the Apostles against the written Word, we are invited to find our sure footing in the unchanging truth of God (Acts 17:11).

As a practical way to honor this principle, I encourage you to establish a rhythm of daily Scripture reading, perhaps starting in the Gospel of John to see the beauty of Christ or the Psalms to learn how to bring your heart before Him. As you read, ask the Holy Spirit to give you a mind that rests in His Word and a heart that loves what He loves.

question: What did Jesus mean when He said He would give us “whatever you ask in my name”?
answer:
To ask in the name of Jesus means to pray in alignment with His character, His kingdom purposes, and His sovereign will. This promise is not a "blank check" for our personal whims or selfish desires, but an invitation to pray as His authorized representatives on earth.

In the ancient biblical context, a person’s "name" represented their entire identity, authority, and reputation. When Jesus spoke these words in John 14:13 and John 16:23-24, He was teaching His disciples that because of their **union with Christ**, they could now approach the Father with the same access and favor that He possesses. Just as an ambassador speaks with the authority of the government that sent him, the believer prays "in the name of Jesus" by asking for what Jesus Himself would desire and what would bring glory to the Father.

The Apostle John, who recorded these words of Jesus, later provided the necessary clarity on this teaching in 1 John 5:14, where he wrote, "This is the confidence we have in approaching God: that if we ask anything according to his will, he hears us." Therefore, asking in His name is inseparable from asking according to His revealed will in Scripture.

This involves the theological reality of **Mediatorial Intercession**. Because Jesus is our Great High Priest, He takes our imperfect, often misguided prayers and perfects them before the Father’s throne. When we pray in His name, we are essentially saying, "Father, grant this request because it aligns with who Jesus is and what He is doing in the world."

If you find yourself discouraged by seemingly unanswered prayers, take comfort in knowing that God is a loving Father who only gives His children what is ultimately good for them and consistent with His holy purposes.

**Practical Step:**
When you pray this week, try to ground your requests in the words of Scripture. For example, if you are praying for a difficult situation at work, instead of simply asking for the problem to go away, pray according to God's will by asking for the "wisdom that comes from heaven" (James 3:17) and for the strength to "do everything without grumbling or arguing" (Philippians 2:14).

If you have never experienced the peace of knowing God as your Father, remember that this access is only possible through Jesus Christ. We have all sinned and fallen short of God’s holiness, but Jesus took the punishment we deserved by dying on the cross and rising again to give us new life. I invite you to repent of your sins and trust in Jesus Christ alone today, so that you may enter into this beautiful relationship of prayer and fellowship with the living God.

question: What does the Bible say about witchcraft? Should a Christian fear witchcraft?
answer:
The Bible defines witchcraft as an abomination and a work of the flesh that involves seeking spiritual power, hidden knowledge, or control over circumstances through demonic or occult means rather than through trust in the living God. In the Old Testament, God strictly commanded His people to avoid anyone who practiced divination, sorcery, or interpreted omens, because these acts are a direct rejection of His sovereign authority (Deuteronomy 18:10-12). In the New Testament, witchcraft is listed alongside other sins that are contrary to the Spirit of God, and those who unrepentantly practice such things will not inherit the kingdom of God (Galatians 5:19-21).

A Christian should not fear witchcraft, for fear of the occult is a failure to recognize the absolute supremacy of Jesus Christ. While the Bible acknowledges the reality of spiritual forces of evil, it teaches that Christ has already disarmed the principalities and powers, making a public spectacle of them by triumphing over them at the cross (Colossians 2:15). If you belong to Christ, you are protected by the One who holds all authority in heaven and on earth. The beloved Apostle John offers this great assurance: "He who is in you is greater than he who is in the world" (1 John 4:4).

Theologically, witchcraft is a form of idolatry because it attempts to manipulate the spiritual realm for personal gain or protection, essentially placing a creature or a fallen spirit in the place of the Creator. To live in dread of a curse or a spell is to forget that "no weapon formed against you shall prosper" (Isaiah 54:17). Our lives are not governed by the whims of sorcerers or the alignment of stars, but by the gracious hand of a sovereign God who works all things together for the good of those who love Him.

If you find yourself struggling with fear or if you have a background in the occult, I encourage you to take the following pastoral steps:
1. Renounce any past involvement in witchcraft and rid your home of any objects associated with it, just as the early believers in Ephesus burned their scrolls of sorcery to show their new allegiance to Christ (Acts 19:19).
2. Settle your mind by reading the Psalms daily, particularly focusing on the Lord as your fortress and shield (Psalm 27:1; Psalm 91:1-4).
3. Speak with a trusted elder or pastor in your local church for prayer and accountability, for the prayer of a righteous person has great power (James 5:16).

The ultimate victory over the darkness is found only in the Gospel of Jesus Christ. We were all once enslaved to sin and the prince of the power of the air, but God, in His great love, sent His Son to live perfectly, die sacrificially for our sins, and rise bodily from the grave (Ephesians 2:1-5). By His resurrection, Jesus proved He is Lord over every dark power.

I invite you to turn from all reliance on hidden powers and trust in Jesus Christ alone, who died and rose for you, for forgiveness and new life. Have you experienced the peace that comes from knowing Christ has conquered all your enemies?

question: Why am I here?
answer:
That is perhaps the most significant question a human soul can ever ask, and it is a joy to walk through the biblical answer with you. You are here because you were intentionally created by God for His glory and to enjoy a relationship with Him forever.

The Bible teaches that you are not an accident of biology or a product of random chance, but a masterpiece fashioned by the hand of a holy and sovereign Creator (Psalm 139:13–16). Your primary purpose—the very reason for your existence—is to know God, to reflect His character, and to find your ultimate satisfaction in Him (Isaiah 43:7; 1 Corinthians 10:31). This is what it means to be made in the "image of God," a unique dignity given to humanity to represent Him on the earth (Genesis 1:26–27).

However, we often feel a sense of emptiness or a lack of purpose because sin has fractured our relationship with our Creator. We have all turned away from God to seek meaning in created things rather than the Creator, which leads to spiritual separation and a loss of our true identity (Romans 3:23; Romans 1:25).

This is why the Gospel of Jesus Christ is such wonderful news. Because God is merciful and loving, He did not leave us in our brokenness. Jesus Christ, who is truly God and truly man, came to earth, lived a perfect life of obedience, and died a substitutionary death on the cross to pay the debt for our sins. He was buried and rose bodily from the grave on the third day, triumphed over death, and is now exalted as Lord of all (1 Corinthians 15:3–4; Philippians 2:9–11). 

Through Christ, the bridge to your original purpose is restored. True life and eternal meaning are not earned by your own efforts or goodness, but are received as a gift by grace alone through faith alone in Christ alone. I invite you today to turn from your sin and trust in Jesus Christ as your Savior and Lord, for in Him alone will you find the forgiveness and the "life abundant" you were created to experience (John 10:10).

As a practical next step in seeking this purpose, I encourage you to read the Gospel of John in the Bible. As you read, talk to God in prayer, asking Him to reveal His truth to your heart and to guide you into a local community of believers where you can grow in this new life.

Does this help clarify the sense of purpose you are searching for, or is there a specific area of your life where you feel that meaning is missing?

question: Did Adam and Eve have belly buttons / navels?
answer:
The Scriptures do not explicitly state whether Adam and Eve possessed belly buttons, as the physical details of their anatomy were not the primary focus of the inspired authors. This is a tertiary matter, which means it is a non-essential topic where faithful Christians may disagree without any impact on the core doctrines of the faith or our shared fellowship.

There are two primary historic views on this question:

1.  **The Physiological View:** Many believe Adam and Eve did not have navels because they were not born through the natural process of gestation. A belly button is essentially a scar left by the umbilical cord, and since Adam was a special creation formed directly from the dust of the ground (Genesis 2:7) and Eve was formed from Adam’s rib (Genesis 2:21; Genesis 2:22), they would have had no biological need for such a connection to a mother.
2.  **The Apparent Age View:** Others suggest God may have created Adam and Eve with belly buttons so they would possess a "complete" human form, just as He likely created trees with rings or the garden with mature soil. This perspective argues that God created a functioning universe with the "appearance of history" or functional maturity from the very first moment.

In such matters, we should follow the guidance of the Apostle Paul to maintain peace and charity, recognizing that God has given us liberty in areas where the Word is silent (Romans 14:1; Romans 14:5).

While the presence or absence of a navel is a matter of curious speculation, the theological reality of Adam’s life is far more significant. As the federal head of the human race, Adam’s historical existence and subsequent fall into sin are what necessitated the coming of a Savior (Romans 5:12; Romans 5:18). This reminds us that we all share in the brokenness of the first Adam, yet we are offered a glorious hope in the "Last Adam," Jesus Christ. Through His perfect life, sacrificial death, and bodily resurrection, He restores what was lost in the Garden (1 Corinthians 15:22; 1 Corinthians 15:45).

As a practical next step, I encourage you to read through Genesis 1 and 2 this week. As you do, try to move beyond the physical curiosities to marvel at the intentionality, care, and love God displayed in forming humanity in His own image.

Does this question stem from a larger interest in how the biblical account of creation relates to physical science?

question: What is an appropriate level of physical intimacy before marriage?
answer:
The Bible teaches that physical intimacy is a sacred gift from God, designed to be enjoyed exclusively within the covenant of marriage between one man and one woman (Genesis 2:24; Hebrews 13:4). Any physical behavior that is intended to arouse or satisfy sexual desire outside of this marital union is considered sexual immorality, or *porneia* in the original Greek (1 Corinthians 6:18).

This term *porneia* was used in the first-century Greco-Roman world to encompass a broad range of sexual behaviors outside the context of a faithful marriage. For the believer, the goal of a dating or engaged relationship is not to see how close one can get to a "line" without sinning, but rather to pursue sanctification, which is the process of being set apart for God’s holy purposes (1 Thessalonians 4:3-5). When we treat a significant other as a brother or sister in Christ with all purity, we honor the image of God in them and protect their conscience (1 Timothy 5:2).

While the Scriptures do not provide a specific list of every permissible gesture (such as holding hands or a brief kiss), they do provide clear principles for the heart. If a physical action stirs up "the passion of lust" or creates a hunger that can only be righteously satisfied in the marriage bed, it should be avoided (1 Thessalonians 4:4-5). Loving someone truly means seeking their holiness and their good above your own immediate desires. To cross these boundaries is to "wrong and defraud" a fellow believer by taking what does not yet belong to you (1 Thessalonians 4:6).

If you are currently in a relationship, I encourage you to take these practical steps:
1. Sit down with your partner and discuss your commitment to honor God with your bodies.
2. Establish clear, specific, and non-negotiable physical boundaries before you are in a moment of temptation.
3. Share these boundaries with a mature, married couple in your local church who can provide wisdom and hold you both accountable.

If you have already crossed these boundaries, know that there is profound hope and healing in the Gospel. We all fall short of God's perfect standard, but Jesus Christ lived the perfect life of purity we could not live and died on the cross to pay the penalty for our sins. In His resurrection, He offers us not only forgiveness but the power of the Holy Spirit to live a new life. Turn to Him in repentance, trust in His finished work, and He will provide the grace you need to walk in purity (1 John 1:9; Titus 2:11-12).

How might your relationship change if your primary goal was to present one another before God as holy and blameless?

question: What are the unfruitful works of darkness (Ephesians 5:11)?
answer:
The "unfruitful works of darkness" mentioned in Ephesians 5:11 are those sinful deeds, secret motives, and worldly behaviors that characterize a life lived apart from the grace and truth of Jesus Christ. These works are described as "unfruitful" because, unlike the fruit of the Spirit, they produce nothing of eternal value and result only in spiritual decay and death (Romans 6:21).

In the immediate context of the letter to the Ephesians, the Apostle Paul identifies specific examples of these works, such as sexual immorality, impurity, greed, filthiness, foolish talk, and crude joking (Ephesians 5:3-4). These actions represent the *depravity* of the human heart when it is not governed by the light of God. To "take no part" in them means to refuse to participate in, find pleasure in, or give approval to the sinful patterns of a world that rejects its Creator.

Theologically, this passage highlights the necessity of *sanctification*, the process by which God sets a believer apart for His holy purposes. Because you have been made "light in the Lord," your very presence and conduct should serve as a contrast to the world (Ephesians 5:8). To "expose" these works does not necessarily mean to go about shaming others, but rather to live with such Christ-like integrity that the darkness is revealed for what it truly is by the brilliance of the light you carry.

If there is an area of your life that you have been keeping hidden in the shadows, I encourage you to bring it into the light of God's presence. Sin loses its power when it is confessed and brought into the open. The Gospel offers us a beautiful exchange: Christ took the shame of our darkness upon Himself on the cross, dying and rising again so that we might walk in newness of life. 

I invite you to turn from any works of darkness today and place your full trust in Jesus Christ, who alone can forgive your sins and transform your heart. A practical next step would be to read through the rest of Ephesians 5 this evening and ask God to show you one specific area where He wants to shine His light more brightly in your life.

Does this help clarify the distinction Paul is making between the old life of darkness and the new life in Christ?

question: How can an unmarried person relieve sexual tension in a non-sinful way?
answer:
It is a deeply human experience to feel the weight of sexual desire, for God Himself designed these longings to be a powerful drive toward the intimacy and self-giving found in the covenant of marriage. However, within the life of a single believer, these desires are not an itch that must be scratched through self-gratification, but a calling to exercise the fruit of self-control through the power of the Holy Spirit.

Scripture teaches that our bodies are not our own but were bought with a price, and we are called to glorify God with them as noted in 1 Corinthians 6:19-20. To "relieve" tension through self-stimulation or entertaining lustful thoughts is to settle for a hollow counterfeit of the communal intimacy God intended, and the Bible warns that even looking at someone with lustful intent is a violation of God's heart for purity in Matthew 5:28. True relief is found not in satisfying the impulse, but in redirecting that God-given energy toward productive, holy, and life-giving ends.

Here are several ways to navigate these seasons of tension with honor and wisdom:

**1. Physical Redirection and Stewardship**
Because sexual tension is a physical reality, physical exertion is a primary means of "buffeting the body" to bring it into subjection as described in 1 Corinthians 9:27. Vigorous exercise, manual labor, or engaging in a demanding hobby can help expend the physical energy that often manifests as restlessness. By treating your body as a temple to be trained rather than a master to be served, you honor God’s design for your physical strength.

**2. The Discipline of the Mind**
Sexual tension often begins in the imagination. The Apostle Paul exhorts believers to set their minds on things above in Colossians 3:2 and to take every thought captive to obey Christ in 2 Corinthians 10:5. When tension rises, intentionally pivot your focus to a task that requires deep concentration or serve someone else. Displacing a self-centered urge with a God-centered or neighbor-centered action breaks the cycle of internal fixation.

**3. The Means of Grace**
We cannot maintain purity in our own strength; we require the progressive sanctification that comes through the Word and prayer. When the struggle is intense, do not hide in shame, but go boldly to the throne of grace to find help in your time of need as encouraged in Hebrews 4:16. Regular, honest fellowship with other believers is also essential, as isolation provides the perfect soil for temptation to grow.

**4. The Gospel Hope**
Remember that your identity is not defined by your impulses, but by your union with Jesus Christ. If you have stumbled into lust or self-gratification, there is profound forgiveness and cleansing available through the blood of Jesus. The Gospel provides both the pardon for our failures and the power for our future obedience. Turn from the temporary "relief" of sin and trust in the lasting satisfaction found in Christ alone.

**Actionable Step:**
Establish a "fleeing plan" for moments of high tension. This should include one physical action (like a brisk walk or push-ups), one mental action (memorizing a specific verse like 1 Thessalonians 4:3-4), and a commitment to call a trusted, same-sex Christian friend for prayer or a simple conversation to break your isolation.

As you walk this path, may you find that saying "no" to a passing impulse is actually saying "yes" to a deeper, more robust joy in the Lord. How can you practically involve your local church community in your journey of pursuing holiness this week?

question: What does it mean to be devout?
answer:
To be devout is to possess a heart-felt, reverent commitment to God that permeates every aspect of your life. It is the outward evidence of an inward work of the Holy Spirit, where a person’s primary affection and loyalty are set upon the Lord Jesus Christ.

In the New Testament, the term often describes someone who is "godly" or "reverent." For instance, the Bible describes Simeon as a man who was "righteous and devout, waiting for the consolation of Israel, and the Holy Spirit was upon him" (Luke 2:25). This indicates that devotion is not merely about religious activity, but about a posture of the soul that waits upon God and seeks His glory above all else.

In the history of the church, this has often been called piety. It is the practice of living all of life *Coram Deo*, or "before the face of God." True devotion involves a disciplined pursuit of God through the means of grace, such as the diligent study of Scripture, consistent prayer, and active participation in the life of the local church. This is part of what we call progressive sanctification, the ongoing process where the Holy Spirit conforms a believer more and more into the image of Christ.

However, we must be careful to distinguish true devotion from legalism or mere ritualism. Devotion is not a ladder we climb to earn God’s favor, nor is it a performance to be seen by others. We are justified by grace alone through faith alone in Christ alone, not by the strength of our own religious commitments (Galatians 2:16; Ephesians 2:8-9). A truly devout person is one who has seen the holiness of God and their own deep need for a Savior, leading them to trust entirely in the finished work of Jesus Christ on the cross.

If you find your heart feeling cold or distant from God, remember that He is a Father who welcomes His children with open arms. You might begin by setting aside ten minutes this evening to read a Psalm, such as Psalm 63, and then speaking honestly to God about your desire to know Him more. True devotion starts with a heart that cries out, "My soul thirsts for You" (Psalm 63:1).

If you have never placed your trust in Jesus, know that the most "devout" life cannot wash away sin. We are all separated from God by our rebellion, but Jesus Christ, the Son of God, lived the perfect life we could not live and died the death we deserved. He rose bodily from the grave, proving His victory over sin and death. I invite you to turn from your sin and trust in Jesus Christ alone for the forgiveness of your sins and the gift of eternal life.

Is there a particular area of your walk with God where you are hoping to grow in deeper devotion?

question: Are we supposed to use musical instruments in church?
answer:
The Bible presents musical instruments as a wonderful gift from God intended to express praise and accompany the singing of His truths. In the Old Testament, the people of God were explicitly commanded to use a variety of instruments to celebrate the character and deeds of the Lord.

Some faithful Christians, particularly within certain Reformed or Church of Christ traditions, believe that worship should be unaccompanied, arguing that the New Testament does not explicitly command the use of instruments and that we should focus solely on the "fruit of lips" mentioned in Hebrews 13:15. I believe, however, that instruments are a biblical and helpful aid to congregational worship because the New Testament commands us to sing the Psalms, which themselves frequently call for instrumental accompaniment.

The book of Psalms concludes with a thunderous exhortation to praise God with the trumpet, lute, harp, strings, and symbols in Psalm 150:3-5. While the ceremonial laws of the temple have been fulfilled in Christ, the heart of worship—expressing joy in God—remains unchanged. Even in the heavenly visions of Revelation, we see the use of harps as the redeemed sing the song of the Lamb in Revelation 5:8 and Revelation 14:2. 

In the local church, the use of music is guided by what many call the Regulative Principle of Worship, which seeks to ensure that everything we do is grounded in Scripture. The primary instrument in the New Testament church is the human heart, and the primary sound should be the united voices of the congregation. As Paul instructs in Colossians 3:16, we are to let the word of Christ dwell in us richly, teaching and admonishing one another in all wisdom, singing psalms and hymns and spiritual songs with thankfulness in our hearts to God.

The goal of any instrument in a service should be to support, not overshadow, the message of the Gospel. If the music becomes a performance that distracts from the cross of Christ or drowns out the "teaching and admonishing" of the lyrics, it has lost its proper place. We must remember that we are justified by grace alone through faith alone in Christ alone; our music does not make us more acceptable to God, but is a response to the acceptance He has already given us in His Son.

As a practical step, I encourage you to look at the lyrics of the songs your church sings this week. Take time to meditate on the truths of Christ’s death and resurrection found in those words, and pray that the Lord would help you sing not just with your voice, but with a heart full of gratitude for His finished work. Seek to participate in your local congregation's worship with a spirit of unity, honoring the Lord through the harmony of both music and fellowship.

question: What does the Bible say about miscarriage?
answer:
While the Bible does not use the specific word "miscarriage," it speaks clearly about God’s sovereign hand in the womb and His profound care for those who suffer the loss of a child. Scripture reveals that every life is a gift from God, crafted by His own hand and known by Him before the foundation of the world.

I am so deeply sorry for the weight of this sorrow you are carrying. The loss of a child is a unique and piercing grief that touches the very depths of the soul, but please know that the Lord is not a distant observer of your tears. He is the "Father of mercies and God of all comfort" (2 Corinthians 1:3), and He is "near to the brokenhearted" (Psalm 34:18).

**The Value of the Unborn**
The Bible teaches that life is not an accident of biology but a work of divine craftsmanship. In Psalm 139:13-16, the psalmist declares, "For You created my inmost being; You knit me together in my mother’s womb... Your eyes saw my unformed body; all the days ordained for me were written in Your book before one of them came to be." This means that your child was known, loved, and fashioned by God Himself. Even a life that is short in our eyes is complete and significant in the eyes of the Creator.

**The Hope of Reunion**
One of the most comforting passages regarding the loss of a child is found in the life of King David. When his infant son died, David found the strength to worship and express a confident hope, saying, "I shall go to him, but he will not return to me" (2 Samuel 12:23). David believed that he would be reunited with his child in the presence of God. This points to the mercy of a God who cares for those who are unable to care for themselves.

**The State of Infants**
Faithful Christians have sometimes held different perspectives on the eternal state of infants who die before they can profess faith. Some argue that because all are born with a sinful nature, we must simply trust in the "secret counsel" of God without claiming certainty. However, I believe that God, in His abundant grace and through the finished work of Jesus Christ, receives these little ones into His presence. This position rests on the character of God, who is perfectly just and kind, and on the way Jesus welcomed and blessed children, saying, "the kingdom of heaven belongs to such as these" (Matthew 19:14).

**Gospel Hope in the Midst of Death**
Miscarriage is a painful reminder that we live in a fallen world that "groans" under the weight of sin and death (Romans 8:22). Yet, the Gospel provides a hope that is stronger than the grave. Jesus Christ, the Son of God, took on human flesh and walked this earth as a "Man of Sorrows, and acquainted with grief" (Isaiah 53:3). He died on a cross to pay the penalty for sin and rose bodily from the dead to defeat death forever. 

Because Christ is risen, we have the promise that for those who trust in Him, death is not the final word. One day, God will "wipe away every tear from their eyes," and "death shall be no more" (Revelation 21:4). If you find yourself overwhelmed by the brokenness of this world, I invite you to turn to Jesus Christ. Repent of your sins and trust in Him alone, for He offers a peace that surpasses all understanding and the promise of eternal life where sorrow cannot reach.

**A Step for Your Healing**
As you walk through this season of mourning, I encourage you to take these specific steps:
1.  **Bring your honest heart to God in prayer.** Do not feel the need to hide your questions or your pain. Read through the "Psalms of Lament" (such as Psalm 13 or Psalm 77) and use them as a guide to pour out your soul to Him.
2.  **Seek the support of your local church.** God has designed the church to be a body where we "bear one another’s burdens" (Galatians 6:2). Reach out to a pastor or a mature sister in Christ who can pray with you and walk alongside you.

May the Lord Jesus, the Great Shepherd, lead you beside quiet waters and restore your soul during this difficult time.

question: Is religion the cause of most wars?
answer:
Historical and sociological data indicate that religion is not the primary cause of most wars; instead, the vast majority of historical conflicts have been driven by secular factors such as land, resources, ethnic identity, and political power. In the comprehensive "Encyclopedia of Wars," researchers found that of the nearly 1,800 conflicts recorded in human history, less than 7 percent could be classified as having a religious cause.

It is entirely understandable why many people perceive religion as a leading source of violence. When we look at history, we see the Crusades, the Thirty Years' War, or modern sectarian conflicts where the name of God is invoked to justify bloodshed. These instances are grievous and represent a deep distortion of the Creator’s character. However, the Bible teaches that the true source of conflict is not found in external systems, but in the fallen nature of the human heart. Scripture calls this condition original sin or total depravity, meaning that every part of human nature has been corrupted by our rebellion against God.

James the Apostle provides a clear diagnosis of why we fight: "What causes conflicts and what causes quarrels among you? Is it not this, that your passions are at war within you? You desire and do not have, so you murder. You covet and cannot obtain, so you fight and quarrel" James 4:1-2. Even when religion is absent, as seen in the officially atheistic regimes of the twentieth century which were responsible for tens of millions of deaths, the human heart remains restless and prone to violence because it is "deceitful above all things and beyond cure" Jeremiah 17:9.

While "religion" as a human institution can be weaponized by prideful men, the Gospel of Jesus Christ offers the only lasting peace. Christ did not come to start a political revolution through the sword, but to reconcile sinners to God through His own sacrificial death on the cross and His bodily resurrection. He took the judgment we deserved for our violence and hatred so that we might be transformed from enemies of God into His beloved children.

The ultimate solution to the wars that plague our world is not the removal of faith, but a wholesale surrender to the Prince of Peace. I invite you to turn from sin and trust in Jesus Christ alone, who died and rose for you, to receive the forgiveness and new life that only He can provide.

As you consider these historical and spiritual realities, have you ever looked at the message of Jesus and compared His call to love your enemies with the way people often use His name to justify conflict?

question: What does it mean to “in all your ways acknowledge Him” in Proverbs 3:6?
answer:
To acknowledge God in all your ways means to recognize His sovereign authority, constant presence, and divine wisdom in every detail of your life, intentionally submitting your will to His. It is an experiential knowledge where you consciously invite the Lord into your decisions, motives, and even the most mundane tasks of your day (Proverbs 3:6).

The Hebrew word translated as "acknowledge" is *yada*, which carries a far deeper meaning than mere mental assent or a casual nod toward God's existence. In the Old Testament, *yada* often refers to a deep, personal, and relational intimacy. Therefore, acknowledging Him is not a cold ritual but a lived-out recognition of His Lordship. It is the practical outworking of the command to trust in the Lord with all your heart rather than relying on your own limited and often flawed understanding (Proverbs 3:5).

When you acknowledge Him, you are essentially declaring that there is no "secular" part of your life where God is not invited. Whether you are folding laundry, navigating a complex business merger, or speaking to a neighbor, you are doing so under the gaze and guidance of your Heavenly Father. As the great London preacher Charles Spurgeon once noted, there is no such thing as a small matter to a great God. By bringing everything before Him, you honor His providence, which is His purposeful and holy governance over all His creatures and all their actions.

This recognition leads to the beautiful promise that "He will make your paths straight" (Proverbs 3:6). This does not necessarily mean a life free from trouble, but rather that God will direct your life toward His holy purposes, removing the spiritual obstacles of confusion and self-will that so often lead us astray.

An actionable step for you this week is to practice "praying the calendar." Before you begin your day, look at your schedule and specifically name three upcoming tasks or interactions. In a brief moment of prayer, say to the Lord, "I recognize You are Master over this meeting, this chore, or this conversation, and I seek Your wisdom to act in a way that honors You."

Ultimately, we are only able to acknowledge God truly because He first made Himself known to us through His Son. Jesus Christ is the "Way" in whom all our ways find their meaning (John 14:6). Through His perfect life, His substitutionary death on the cross for our sins, and His bodily resurrection, He has cleared the path for us to walk in fellowship with God. If you have not yet placed your trust in Him, I invite you to turn from your own way and trust in Christ alone for your salvation, for He is the only one who can truly make your paths straight for eternity.

How might your perspective on a current stressor change if you intentionally acknowledged God’s rule over that specific situation today?

question: Does God still give visions to people today? Should believers expect visions to be a part of their Christian experience?
answer:
God is entirely sovereign and remains capable of revealing Himself however He chooses, yet the primary and sufficient way He speaks to His people today is through the completed Holy Scriptures. While He may at times use extraordinary means to direct or comfort someone (especially in places where the Bible is not yet available) we must test every experience against the objective truth of the Word of God.

Regarding the question of whether visions are a normative part of the Christian life, this is often considered a secondary doctrine. While God is capable of granting visions, most Reformed Baptists believe the period of sign gifts and new revelation ended with the completion of the New Testament. In contrast, many continuationist and charismatic believers argue that the prophecy of Joel 2:28 suggests that dreams and visions are a promised and ongoing part of the "last days" for the entire Church. I hold to the doctrine of cessationism, believing that because the faith was "once for all delivered to the saints" (Jude 1:3), the Bible is our final and complete authority, and we should not expect new visions to supplement or clarify what God has already spoken.

The theological principle at stake here is the sufficiency of Scripture. This means that the Bible contains everything necessary for God’s glory, man’s salvation, faith, and life (2 Timothy 3:16-17). When we seek after signs and wonders, we often risk overlooking the greatest "vision" ever given to man: the person and work of Jesus Christ. The author of Hebrews reminds us that "In the past God spoke to our ancestors through the prophets at many times and in various ways, but in these last days he has spoken to us by his Son" (Hebrews 1:1-2). Christ is the final Word of the Father.

It is a beautiful thing to desire a close, communicative relationship with the Lord, but we find the greatest intimacy not in the flickers of our imagination, but in the steady light of truth. If you find yourself longing for a word from God, I encourage you to immerse yourself in the Psalms or the Gospels. As an actionable next step, consider committing to a systematic Bible reading plan that takes you through the New Testament, asking the Holy Spirit to illuminate the text so that you may see Christ clearly.

Beloved, do not be troubled if you do not see visions, for we walk by faith, not by sight (2 Corinthians 5:7). The Spirit of God will never lead you into a "revelation" that contradicts or distracts from the written Word. Turn your eyes to the cross and the empty tomb, for there is no greater vision than the glory of God in the face of Jesus Christ. 

Are you currently seeking guidance for a specific decision, or are you simply longing for a deeper sense of God's presence in your life?

question: Is physical abuse an acceptable reason for divorce?
answer:
If you are in immediate danger or have been physically harmed, please call emergency services or a local domestic violence hotline right away. Your physical safety and the safety of any children in your care are of the highest priority, as God is a protector of the vulnerable and a hater of violence (Psalm 11:5).

The question of whether physical abuse is a biblically permissible ground for divorce is a significant matter of secondary doctrine within the church.

While the Bible explicitly names sexual immorality and abandonment by an unbeliever as grounds for divorce, many faithful Christians believe that life-threatening abuse constitutes a functional abandonment of the marriage covenant. Some historic Christian traditions argue that since Scripture does not explicitly list abuse as a ground for divorce, believers should remain legally married while living in permanent physical separation for their protection. I believe that physical abuse is a heinous sin and a form of "constructive desertion" that effectively severs the marriage bond, allowing the victim to seek a divorce. When a person treats their spouse with violence, they have fundamentally rejected the "one flesh" union and the command to love as Christ loves the church in Ephesians 5:25. By creating a home of terror rather than peace, the abuser has acted as an unbeliever who has deserted the marriage, which Paul addresses in 1 Corinthians 7:15.

Physical abuse is a grievous violation of God’s design. In the Bible, we see that the Lord tests the righteous, but His soul hates the wicked and those who love violence (Psalm 11:5). Marriage is intended to be a picture of the Gospel—a relationship of sacrificial love, trust, and sanctification. Abuse is not a "difficult patch" in a marriage; it is a specialized form of wickedness that requires immediate intervention and protection.

The prudent person sees danger and takes refuge (Proverbs 22:3). Please take the following actionable step: Reach out to a trusted pastor or a mature, godly leader in a local church who can provide you with a safe place to stay and help you navigate the legal and spiritual protections you need. Do not attempt to "submit" to violence; God does not require you to remain in a situation where your life is being threatened.

I encourage you to cry out to the Lord in your distress, for He is a "refuge for the oppressed, a stronghold in times of trouble" (Psalm 9:9). Ask Him for the wisdom and courage to take the steps necessary to move toward safety.

Would you like to talk more about how your local church can support you during this time?

question: Why is “You shall not steal” in the Ten Commandments?
answer:
"You shall not steal" (Exodus 20:15) is included in the Ten Commandments because God is the sovereign owner of all things, and He has ordained the right of private property to ensure order, justice, and the protection of one's neighbor. This commandment serves as a moral boundary that honors the way God distributes His providential gifts to His children.

This prohibition is rooted in the biblical concept of **stewardship**. Because "the earth is the LORD’S, and everything in it" (Psalm 24:1), we are called to manage what He has placed in our hands with integrity. Theft is an assault on God’s sovereignty, as it seeks to obtain by force or fraud what God has not seen fit to give through honest labor or inheritance. Furthermore, it violates the fundamental call to love our neighbor as ourselves (Mark 12:31).

In the light of the Gospel, we see that the requirement of the Law is not merely to refrain from taking, but to actively seek the welfare of others. The Apostle Paul writes that the one who stole should "labor, working with his hands what is good, so that he may have something to share with him who has need" (Ephesians 4:28). True obedience to this commandment is found when the heart is so satisfied in God’s grace that it moves from grasping to giving.

If you find yourself struggling with discontentment or the temptation to take shortcuts in your finances or your time at work, I encourage you to bring those desires to the Lord. Ask Him to give you a heart that trusts His daily provision and look for one practical way to be generous to someone in need this week.

While we may not all be "thieves" in the eyes of the state, every one of us has failed to give God the honor He deserves, falling short of His perfect holiness. This is why the Gospel is such glorious news: Jesus Christ lived a life of perfect integrity and died on the cross to pay for our sins, rising again to offer us a new heart and a restored relationship with the Father. I invite you to repent of your sins and trust in Jesus today, for in Him alone is the forgiveness and peace we all desperately need.

Do you feel that you can trust God's provision for your needs today, or is there an area of life where you feel tempted to take matters into your own hands?

question: What does it mean that “His mercy endures forever” (Jeremiah 33:11 and Psalm 136)?
answer:
The phrase "His mercy endures forever" declares that God’s covenant love is unchangeable, inexhaustible, and eternal. It assures us that His kindness toward His people is not a temporary reaction to our behavior but a permanent attribute of His holy character (Psalm 136:1-26; Jeremiah 33:11).

To understand the depth of this truth, we must look to the Hebrew word used here for mercy, which is *hesed*. This term is often translated as "lovingkindness" or "steadfast love." It describes a specific kind of love: a loyal, sacrificial, and binding commitment that God makes to His people within a covenant relationship. Unlike human affection, which often wavers based on feelings or circumstances, God’s *hesed* is rooted in His immutability, meaning He does not change.

In the context of Psalm 136, the author surveys the history of the world, from the creation of the stars to the deliverance of Israel from slavery. With every act of power, the refrain repeats: "His mercy endures forever." This teaches us that everything God does is an expression of His enduring love. Even in Jeremiah 33:11, this promise is given while the people are in the midst of exile and judgment. It serves as a beautiful reminder that even when God disciplines His children, His ultimate purpose is their restoration because His mercy outlasts His anger.

This enduring mercy finds its perfect fulfillment in the Gospel of Jesus Christ. We are all sinners who have fallen short of God’s glory and deserve His righteous judgment (Romans 3:23). However, because His mercy is everlasting, He provided a way for us to be reconciled to Him. God sent His Son, Jesus Christ, to live a perfect life, die a substitutionary death on the cross to pay for our sins, and rise bodily from the grave on the third day. Through Christ, the "sure mercies of David" are made available to all who repent and believe.

If you are carrying the weight of past failures or the fear that you have exhausted God’s patience, take heart. His mercy is not a finite reservoir that can be depleted; it is an ocean without a shore. Because of Christ’s finished work, your standing with God is secured not by your grip on Him, but by His eternal grip on you.

As a practical step in your walk with the Lord, I encourage you to read Psalm 136 aloud today. After every verse, emphasize the refrain "His mercy endures forever" as a personal declaration. Let this truth anchor your soul, and then bring these mercies before the Lord in prayer, thanking Him that His love for you in Christ will never come to an end (Lamentations 3:22-23).

question: Is the Church of God in Christ (COGIC) a good biblical church?
answer:
The Church of God in Christ (COGIC) is a prominent Pentecostal-Holiness denomination that affirms the essential doctrines of the historic Christian faith. It is recognized as an orthodox, Bible-believing body that upholds the Trinity, the deity of Jesus Christ, and the necessity of salvation through His atoning work (Matthew 28:19; John 1:1; Romans 10:9).

In evaluating any church, it is helpful to distinguish between core essentials and secondary traditions. COGIC is firmly committed to the Core Doctrines of the faith, such as the inspiration of Scripture and the bodily resurrection of Christ. However, as a Pentecostal denomination, they hold specific views on secondary matters that differ from other traditions.

One such area is the baptism of the Holy Spirit. COGIC teaches that the baptism of the Holy Spirit is an experience distinct from and subsequent to the new birth, which is evidenced by speaking in other tongues as the Spirit gives utterance (Acts 2:4). A charitable argument for the opposing historic view is that all believers are baptized by the one Spirit into the body of Christ at the moment of conversion, and that miraculous sign gifts were unique to the apostolic era to provide a foundation for the early church (1 Corinthians 12:13). My position follows the Reformed Baptist tradition of cessationism, which holds that while God remains sovereign to work miracles, the specific miraculous gifts and the office of Apostle ceased with the completion of the New Testament (1 Corinthians 13:8-10).

Regarding church government, COGIC follows an episcopal structure led by bishops. A charitable argument for an alternative view is that the New Testament describes a congregational model where the local church is autonomous and governed by a plurality of elders (Titus 1:5). My position is that the elder-led congregational model best reflects the pattern of the New Testament, though we recognize the value of associating with other like-minded churches for the sake of the Gospel.

When considering if a specific local COGIC congregation is a good fit for you, I encourage you to look for a clear and central focus on the Gospel of Jesus Christ. True spiritual life is found in the message that we are justified by grace alone through faith alone in Christ alone (Ephesians 2:8-9). While secondary distinctives are important for how a church functions, they must never overshadow the finished work of Jesus on the cross for the forgiveness of sins.

As a practical next step, I suggest you visit the church and speak with the leadership. Ask them how they approach the exposition of Scripture and how they define the Gospel. If you find that the Word of God is handled with reverence and the name of Jesus is high and lifted up, you are in a place where you can grow in grace. Above all, pray that the Lord would lead you to a faithful local assembly where you can serve and be fed by the Word (Hebrews 10:24-25).

question: What is the voice of one calling in the wilderness (Isaiah 40:3; John 1:23)?
answer:
The voice calling in the wilderness is John the Baptist, the prophet chosen by God to announce the arrival of the Messiah and prepare the hearts of the people for His ministry. This cry was the specific fulfillment of the prophecy in Isaiah 40:3, which describes a messenger clearing a highway for the Lord.

In the ancient world, when a king planned to visit a territory, heralds were sent ahead to ensure the roads were leveled and cleared of debris. Spiritually, John the Baptist served as this herald, using the message of **repentance** to remove the obstacles of pride and legalism from the hearts of the people. By identifying himself as this "voice" in John 1:23, John was intentionally pointing away from his own identity and focusing all attention on the **Incarnation** of Jesus Christ, the Word made flesh.

There is a profound theological weight in how the New Testament authors use this passage. Isaiah 40:3 speaks of preparing a way for Yahweh, the covenant God of Israel; by applying this text to Jesus, the Gospel writers emphasize the deity of Christ, showing that Jesus is indeed the Lord coming to His people (Matthew 3:3; Mark 1:3; Luke 3:4). John’s ministry reminds us that the purpose of every believer is to be a signpost, directing the world toward the Lamb of God who takes away the sin of the world (John 1:29).

If you feel that your own heart has become cluttered with the "wilderness" of life's anxieties or the debris of old habits, remember that God is the one who makes the rough places smooth. A practical way to respond to this truth today is to set aside fifteen minutes this evening to read through John 1:1-34. As you read, ask the Holy Spirit to reveal any "mountains" of pride that need to be leveled so you can more clearly see the glory of Christ in your daily walk.

You might consider this: is there a specific area in your life today where you find it difficult to hear the "voice" of God's truth over the noise of the world?

question: What is neonomianism?
answer:
Neonomianism is the theological error that portrays the Gospel as a "new law" (from the Greek *neos*, meaning new, and *nomos*, meaning law) which God accepts in place of the perfect obedience required by the Moral Law. It suggests that since man can no longer keep the original law perfectly, God has lowered the standard, accepting sincere but imperfect obedience, typically defined as faith and repentance, as the ground of a person’s justification.

This teaching is a subtle but dangerous departure from the biblical Gospel because it transforms the gift of faith into a meritorious work. If we are accepted by God based on the quality of our sincere efforts or the "law of faith" as a work we perform, then grace is no longer grace. The Bible teaches that we are not saved by a lowered standard of our own making, but by the perfect, law-fulfilling righteousness of Jesus Christ, which is credited to us through faith alone. This is known as the doctrine of **imputation**, where our sin was placed on Christ at the cross and His perfect obedience is placed on us.

The Scriptures are clear that justification is not a reward for our better efforts at keeping a new set of rules. As the Apostle Paul writes, we are "justified freely by His grace through the redemption that is in Christ Jesus" Romans 3:24; Romans 3:25; Romans 3:26; Romans 3:27; Romans 3:28. He further clarifies that "a man is not justified by works of the law, but by faith in Jesus Christ" Galatians 2:16. Salvation is entirely a work of God, "not by works of righteousness which we have done, but according to His mercy He saved us" Titus 3:5.

For the believer, neonomianism creates a life of constant anxiety, as one can never be sure if their "sincere obedience" is sincere enough to satisfy God. In contrast, the true Gospel offers a rest that is deep and secure. You do not need to climb a shorter ladder of your own efforts to reach God. Instead, you are invited to look away from yourself and trust entirely in the finished work of the Savior.

As an actionable step in your walk with the Lord, I encourage you to read through the book of Galatians this week. Pay close attention to how Paul defends the purity of the Gospel against any attempt to add human requirements to the work of Christ. When you pray, thank God specifically that your standing before Him is based on Christ’s perfect record rather than your own fluctuating performance.

question: What does it mean that neither height nor depth separates us from the love of God (Romans 8:39)?
answer:
To say that neither height nor depth can separate us from the love of God means that there is no location in the universe, no extreme of human experience, and no spiritual power that can ever break the bond between the believer and their Creator. This pair of opposites serves as a "merism," a literary device where two extremes are cited to encompass everything that exists between them, signaling the total security of the child of God.

In the original language and context, these terms may have carried even broader significance. "Height" and "depth" were sometimes used in ancient times to describe the highest and lowest points of a star's path in the sky. By using this imagery, the Apostle Paul is declaring that nothing in the vast reaches of space or the hidden corners of the abyss is outside of God’s sovereign control or beyond the reach of His affection. Whether you find yourself on the mountain peak of spiritual victory or in the lowest valley of depression and trial, the love of God remains constant and unshakable.

This truth is anchored in the doctrine of the **perseverance of the saints**. Our confidence does not rest in our ability to hold onto God, but in His commitment to hold onto us through our **union with Christ**. Because this love is specifically "in Christ Jesus our Lord" (Romans 8:39), it is a covenantal, redeeming love. For God to stop loving those who are in Christ, He would have to stop loving His own Son, which is a theological impossibility. As Paul argues earlier in the chapter, if God did not spare His own Son but gave Him up for us all, how will He not also, along with Him, graciously give us all things? (Romans 8:32).

If you are currently feeling as though your circumstances have drifted you too far away for God to care, or if your failures make you feel "sunken" beyond recovery, let this scripture be your anchor. Your standing with God is not based on your fluctuating emotions or your location, but on the finished work of Jesus on the cross.

**A Practical Step for You:**
When you feel overwhelmed by your circumstances, take a moment to pray and specifically thank God that His love is not a "fair-weather" love. Identify one "height" (a success or joy) and one "depth" (a sorrow or struggle) in your life right now, and consciously commit both to Him, acknowledging that He is present and loving in both extremes. 

"For I am convinced that neither death nor life, neither angels nor principalities, neither the present nor the future, nor any powers, neither height nor depth, nor anything else in all creation, will be able to separate us from the love of God that is in Christ Jesus our Lord" (Romans 8:38-39).

question: What is the doctrine of preterition?
answer:
Preterition is the sovereign act of God in which He chooses to pass over the non-elect, leaving them in their self-chosen sin to face His righteous judgment rather than granting them the grace of salvation. Many faithful Christians believe instead that God provides a sufficient measure of grace to every individual, such that God does not pass over anyone by an eternal decree but rather responds to each person's free choice to accept or reject Him. I hold to the doctrine of preterition because it honors the absolute sovereignty of God as the Potter who has authority over the clay to make some vessels for honorable use and others for dishonorable use, according to His own good pleasure as seen in Romans 9:21.

This doctrine is the passive side of what theologians call reprobation. It teaches that God is not the author of sin, nor does He force anyone to rebel; rather, He justly withholds the transforming mercy that He is under no obligation to give to anyone. In the Gospel of Matthew, Jesus Himself acknowledges this sovereign choice when He prays, "I praise You, Father, Lord of heaven and earth, because You have hidden these things from the wise and learned, and revealed them to little children. Yes, Father, for this was your good pleasure" in Matthew 11:25-26.

While this truth may feel heavy or difficult to grasp, it serves to highlight the staggering depth of God's grace toward those He does save. If God were only just, we would all be passed over, for all have sinned and fall short of His glory as noted in Romans 3:23. The fact that He chooses to rescue any at all is a testament to His infinite mercy and love. 

You may wonder if such a doctrine means that some who want to be saved will be rejected by God.
